MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Aug-01-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Nathaniel Leeds, Esq. 310-592-3353 of Mitchell Leeds for Plaintiff.
Robert Willoughby, Esq. 415-288-9800 of Hassard Bonnington for Defendant Regents of UC.
Elisabeth Madden, Esq. 415-851-5900 of Madden & Lynch for Defendant Chanfra Ellis.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares its views. Defendant Regents of UC argues there is no breach on standard of care and requests to continue. Defendant Ellis argues. Plaintiff does not contest but argues.

(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Defendant The Regents of the University of California's motion for summary judgment is denied.

"'When a defendant moves for summary judgment and supports his motion with expert declarations that his conduct fell within the community standard of care, he is entitled to summary judgment unless the plaintiff comes forward with conflicting expert evidence.'" (Munro v. Regents of University of California (1989) 215 Cal.App.3d 977, 985.) In this case, the declaration of Dr. McGuire shows that The Regents' doctors were acting in a consultative fashion and complied with the standard of care. (McGuire Decl. 8, 9.) In opposition, the declaration of Dr. Frankel creates a triable issue of material fact regarding whether they breached the standard of care by not sufficiently following up on decedent Geovonni Mabrey's care and by advising against the use of antibiotics. (Frankel Decl. 7-12.) "[W]hen considering the declarations of the parties' experts, we liberally construe the declarations for the [opposing party's] experts and resolve any doubts as to the propriety of granting the motion in favor of the [opposing party]." (Powell v. Kleinman (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 112, 125-126 [reversing summary judgment for defendants in medical malpractice case where opposing party's expert declaration established triable issues of material fact on breach of standard of care and causation].) There are also triable issues of fact as to causation. (Compare McGuire Decl. 9 with Frankel Decl. 11.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)
